name: New Hampshire Bulletin
title: North Conway region considers investment in trash juice treatment
description: With truckloads of trash juice laden with toxic PFAS arriving at its facility, a wastewater treatment plant in North Conway is considering investing in emerging technology to help remove the “forever chemicals.” Other towns in New Hampshire are exploring the technology, which is already used by some private companies, including Casella Waste Systems, to redu[...]
text:
A foam fractionation unit operated by Allonnia, a Boston-based environmental engineering company, at a treatment site in California. Allonnia was one of three vendors evaluated by the North Conway Water Precinct for a possible treatment system to target PFAS in trash juice on site. Voters will consider whether to invest in long-term deployment of the technology at the North Conway facility this spring. (Photo courtesy Allonnia)With truckloads of trash juice laden with toxic PFAS arriving at its facility, a wastewater treatment plant in North Conway is considering investing in emerging technology to help remove the “forever chemicals.”
Other towns in New Hampshire are exploring the technology, which is already used by some private companies, including Casella Waste Systems, to reduce the PFAS load in wastewater. But if North Conway secures voter approval later this month to invest about $9 million in the project, it would set in motion the first long-term, municipal deployment of the technology in New Hampshire, according to North Conway Water Precinct Superintendent Jason Gagnon.
Environmental groups have previously pointed out that the method, called foam fractionation, isn’t a blanket solution. In laboratory studies, it has shown different rates of success with different types of PFAS, and when deployed alone, it is not enough to destroy the chemicals. Nonetheless, with a series of on-site trials yielding promising results, Gagnon said he hoped the technology could help the municipality take a more active role in targeting the flow of PFAS through its facility into the environment.
Concentrating a diffuse threat
Forever chemicals are widespread in manufacturing and consumer goods, from food packaging to plastics and clothes. The moniker refers to their resistance to breaking down, a trait that makes the chemicals difficult to destroy or convert into a less-dangerous form through conventional treatments.
The chemicals accumulate in the environment, where they are widespread, and in the human body, where they are linked to serious health impacts like elevated risk of birth defects and certain cancers. There is no safe level of exposure to PFAS chemicals, according to the U.S. EPA.
PFAS arrive at wastewater treatment plants in sewage, stormwater, industrial wastewater, and leachate, the “trash juice” that is regularly pumped out of landfills and taken to treatment plants for processing. Because traditional wastewater processing does not remove the chemicals, they often pass right through the facility, later to be released into the environment through treated water and the biosolids known as sludge.
For Conway’s water treatment plant, like others that accept leachate, trash juice is among the most potent sources of PFAS in its system. At the North Conway Water Precinct, although leachate makes up about 2% to 3% of the annual liquid volume handled at the plant, just that amount contributes about half of the total PFAS that pass through the facility in a year, according to Gagnon.
For this reason, North Conway chose to focus the pilot project on leachate treatment, rather than attempting to treat the whole volume of wastewater handled at the plant.
“If we were going to treat that huge amount of volume that comes into the sewer pipes … we would need to treat 260 million gallons a year. It would never be cost effective,” he said. “But because the landfill leachate is such a big source [of PFAS] and it’s a small volume, we can do a project to treat it at a much lower cost.”
Toxic bubbles
Even narrowing down treatment to encompass only leachate, the few processes that actually destroy PFAS molecules would not be practical for the North Conway Water Precinct to apply to such a volume, Gagnon said. Instead, the goal of the pilot was to concentrate and separate PFAS from leachate. That’s where foam fractionation comes in.
In this process, air is pumped through a column of PFAS-contaminated water, filling it with streams of air termed “bubble swarms” in a 2024 review of the technology published in the Journal of Hazardous Materials. 
Many, though not all, PFAS molecules have properties similar to soaps, detergents, and other members of a chemical group called surfactants. When dissolved in water, these chemicals naturally gather in a layer on the surface where air and water meet. 
Introducing more air and movement into such a mixture, as “bubble swarms” do, increases the contact area between air and water. This allows more of the molecules to be drawn out of the water as they form bubbles and rise to the surface, where they can be skimmed off.
The process is the same as the one through which soapy water foams in a bathtub or a kitchen sink, though with some additional challenges, such as a lower concentration of PFAS in wastewater or leachate than, for example, of soap in typical dishwater. To help overcome that, additional surfactants can be added to the solution to boost its foaminess or the treatment time can be extended, according to the review.
This process yields highly PFAS-concentrated foam, called foamate, which collapses back down into a liquid when the bubbles burst.
Results may vary
How effective is foam fractionation? In laboratory studies, the method has shown mixed success with different types of molecules; there are thousands of different PFAS chemicals. 
The process can be highly effective at capturing certain long PFAS, like PFOA and PFOS, according to the 2024 review. But other molecules aren’t susceptible to capture with this technique and end up slipping by, wrote Conservation Law Foundation staff attorney Nora Boswell in a 2024 statement. 
This includes smaller molecules, which are more soluble in water, and PFAS that do not have the chemical properties conducive to foaming.
Of the thousands of PFAS found in leachate, scientists who worked on North Conway’s pilot program focused on seven chemicals as a sample group, according to Gagnon.
The precinct partnered with three vendor companies: Water and Carbon Group, based in New Jersey; E2 Metrix, based in Quebec, Canada; and Massachusetts-based Allonia. Each brought mobile equipment to the North Conway water treatment plant and processed leachate on site as it arrived from the Conway Municipal Landfill, Gagnon said. They measured the concentration of target PFAS compounds in the leachate before, during, and after treatment. 
Final results are not yet in, Gagnon said. But early data shows that all seven of those target chemicals were removed sufficiently to bring the leachate concentration down to target levels, he said. 
Based on factors like molecule size, the groups aimed for different levels of concentration for different chemicals, ranging from 132 parts per trillion at the high end to “non-detect” at the low end, meaning laboratory equipment cannot find the chemical in a sample.
After the process is complete, there is also the matter of what happens to the resulting PFAS-rich foam.
Gagnon said the North Conway Water Precinct would likely ship its foam somewhere to be processed if this pathway is pursued for the long term. 
Concentrated PFAS foamate can be subjected to the high-temperature and high-pressure processes that help destroy the chemicals, he said, or captured in cement intended to prevent the chemicals from leaching out.
However, responding to Casella Waste System’s plan to utilize the technology at a Vermont landfill, the Conservation Law Foundation said there was insufficient evidence of the effectiveness of both foam fractionation and the ability of concrete to keep PFAS contained long-term.
Barriers
Gagnon said he was encouraged by the preliminary results of the pilot program and was interested in seeing the equipment fully installed in North Conway.
“The whole reason the wastewater plant exists is to keep those contaminants out of the drinking water, out of the groundwater, so anything we can do to remove those chemicals before we discharge the treated wastewater back into the groundwater, that’s important to us,” he said.
For leachate treatment, foam fractionation is preferable to other methods used to target PFAS in water, such as the granulated carbon often used in drinking water treatment. That’s because wastewater and leachate are so enriched with other pollutants, waste, and more that the carbon filter would get bogged down fast, Gagnon said. 
“Anything you ever threw away, it’s in the landfill leachate,” he said.
However, like many means of treating water for PFAS, foam fractionation is expensive. To treat only leachate, the North Conway Water Precinct would need to invest about $9 million, a sum they will ask voters for this spring, Gagnon said. The precinct encompasses Conway, parts of North Conway, and lower Bartlett.
In addition to addressing health concerns, the investment could help counteract another point of tension for the water precinct: The rising cost of disposing of sludge, owing to mounting concern among the public about its toxic PFAS content.
“People are concerned about PFAS,” he said. “… I think everybody sees the value in making sure that we’re taking as much of this stuff out of the wastewater as we can, but it does come down to cost at the end of the day.”

name: South Carolina Daily Gazette
title: SC environmental regulators investigating chemical spill at solar panel manufacturer
description: COLUMBIA — South Carolina’s environmental agency halted incoming chemical shipments pending an investigation at a controversial solar panel manufacturing plant Tuesday following a spill at the recently opened site in York County. Silfab Solar spilled 300 gallons of potassium hydroxide solution at its location near the Charlotte suburb of Fort Mill, less than[...]
text:
South Carolina's environmental agency halted chemical shipments to Silfab Solar's solar panel manufacturing plant in York County following a spill Tuesday, March 3, 2026, at the recently opened site. (File photo/Getty Images)COLUMBIA — South Carolina’s environmental agency halted incoming chemical shipments pending an investigation at a controversial solar panel manufacturing plant Tuesday following a spill at the recently opened site in York County.
Silfab Solar spilled 300 gallons of potassium hydroxide solution at its location near the Charlotte suburb of Fort Mill, less than 2 miles from the North Carolina border.
The odorless chemical is used in a number of household products, including paint removers and drain cleaner. In solar panel production, the chemical dissolved in water makes it easier for solar panels to absorb the light needed for energy production.
The company contained most of the chemical spill but some reached a nearby stormwater retention pond, according to a letter sent to the company by Myra Reece, director of the state Department of Environmental Services.
Silfab, in a statement following the spill, said it notified county emergency management and there was no danger to public safety.
Still, a pair of state senators who represent York County residents called for state regulators to step in and investigate. While the company has yet to begin operations at the facility, officials had cleared it to begin stocking up on the necessary chemicals.
“Given that such an incident occurred a mere two weeks after Silfab was approved to bring these chemicals in for operations, we have grave concerns about the professionalism of the company and their ability to both recognize and adhere to state regulations put in place to protect members of our community,” state Sens. Michael Johnson, of Tega Cay, and Wes Climer, of Rock Hill, wrote in a joint statement.
The two Republicans said Reece had their “utmost support in remedying this matter by any and all means available.”
That did little to assuage longtime opponents of the site.
Residents have pushed back against Silfab locating on the property due to the use of hazardous chemicals, including hydrochloric acid, anhydrous ammonia, silane and hydroflouric acid, in its solar cell- and panel-making process. Uses for these chemicals include rust removal, fertilizer, herbicides and waterproofing, to name a few.
Some 8,000 people signed a petition opposing the site, according to Scott Jenson who leads the citizen group calling itself Move Silfab.
The opponents argue county officials should not have allowed the company to manufacture solar cells in a light industrial area and even commissioned a study, conducted by University South Carolina professors, modeling the potential health risks.
Jenson said he was “disheartened” and “frustrated” by news of the spill but wasn’t necessarily surprised.
“I hope this a wakeup call for the public,” he said. “Get educated because this is not theoretical anymore. This is real life.”
Jenson went on to say he has little confidence that any penalties environmental investigators may impose will be more than a slap on the wrist
Residents and groups have filed multiple ongoing lawsuits against Silfab, the state Department of Environmental Services and York County over the permitting process that allowed for the development to happen.
Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette, who is a Republican candidate for governor, also waded into the fray Tuesday.
“Regardless of what caused this leak and its ultimate resolution, residents and parents deserve answers,” she wrote on social media.
“Silfab must be held accountable — providing assurances that these chemicals are properly contained, will not cause harm, and that adequate plans are in place for future spills or emergencies.”
Previously, Republican U.S. Rep. Ralph Norman, another gubernatorial candidate whose congressional district covers Silfab’s plant, hosted an informational session between residents and state officials in 2024 amid the site’s controversy.

name: Ohio Capital Journal
title: Ohio U.S. House members have mixed reactions across party lines on Trump’s Iran military strikes
description: Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Greg Landsman, of Cincinnati, joined many Ohio Republicans in defending President Donald Trump’s decision to join Israel’s deadly attack in Iran while Ohio Republican U.S. Rep. Warren Davidson, of Troy, joined several Ohio Democrats in criticizing Trump for not seeking congressional authority.  The Iranian Red Crescent Society said[...]
text:
The U.S. Capitol. (Photo by Jennifer Shutt/States Newsroom)  Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Greg Landsman, of Cincinnati, joined many Ohio Republicans in defending President Donald Trump’s decision to join Israel’s deadly attack in Iran while Ohio Republican U.S. Rep. Warren Davidson, of Troy, joined several Ohio Democrats in criticizing Trump for not seeking congressional authority. 
The Iranian Red Crescent Society said the attacks on 131 cities have killed at least 555 people so far in the Islamic Republic. Iran Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in the military strikes over the weekend. Six U.S. service members have been killed as of Monday afternoon, according to the U.S. Central Command. 
SUBSCRIBE: GET THE MORNING HEADLINES DELIVERED TO YOUR INBOX
“The U.S. is destroying Iran’s missiles and bombs to stop them from taking more lives,” Landsman said in a statement. 
“I hope these targeted strikes on the Iranian regime’s military assets ends the regime’s mayhem and bloodshed and makes way for this lasting peace in the region.” 
Davidson said last week he will support Virginia Democratic U.S. Sen. Tim Kaine’s War Powers Resolution, which would prevent continued U.S. military action in Iran without congressional approval under the 1973 War Powers Resolution. 
“War requires Congressional authorization,” Davidson said on X. “There are actions short of war, but no case has been made.”
Congress holds the sole authority to formally declare war and they are scheduled to vote this week on a bipartisan war powers resolution designed to curb Trump’s military operations in Iran. 
Ohio Gov. Mike DeWine said he would not comment on Trump not getting congressional approval before the military strike. 
“One of the things we know is that the regime in Iran was a horrible regime,” DeWine said to reporters on Monday. 
“These are ruthless, horrible people who are killing thousands of their own people because they wanted to demonstrate that. They wanted to make their voices heard. No one should shed any tears for the people in the leadership of the country who were killed.” 
Ohio Democratic Congress members react
Many Ohio Democratic Congress members criticized Trump for not seeking congressional authority. 
“Once again, President Trump has launched illegal military strikes on a foreign country without congressional authorization,” Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Emilia Sykes, of Akron, said in a statement. 
“These attacks escalate tensions in the Middle East, put American service members at risk, and endanger civilians in Iran. … Military action without clear objectives or a legal mandate is reckless and unconstitutional.” 
The American people do not want another war, said Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Shontel Brown, of Warrensville Heights. 
“This President promised to end wars, not start news,” Brown said on X. “Yet over the last year, we have seen the same pattern: more strikes, in more places, with less explanation and less accountability.” 
Trump ordered airstrikes in Venezuela and the capture of their president earlier this year. 
Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Joyce Beatty, of Columbus, called Trump’s actions “reckless,” saying they put U.S. soldiers in danger. 
“The safety of Americans must come first: Congress must quickly vote on the War Powers Resolution to ensure American service members are removed from harm’s way and to keep America out of another war in the Middle East,” Beatty said on X. 
Ohio Democratic U.S. Rep. Marcy Kaptur, of Toledo, said U.S. citizens have a right to hear Congress debate before troops are deployed and tax dollars are used. 
“This military action will precipitate long-lasting repercussions across a vast region of the Middle East, likely eliciting long term reactions and engagements,” Kaptur said on X. “The American people deserve to know when the lives of our valiant forces and countless innocent civilians are being risked and extended halfway around the world.” 
Ohio Republican Congress members react 
Both of Ohio’s U.S. Senators praised Trump’s actions. 
“President Trump sought for months to avoid conflict and negotiate with Iran to prevent them from rebuilding their nuclear program,” Ohio Republican U.S. Sen. Bernie Moreno said on X. “I fully support his decision to eliminate the threat once and for all once it became clear there was no other option.” 
For 47 years, the Iranian regime had ample opportunity to be a productive member of the global community, Ohio Republican U.S. Sen. Jon Husted said on X. 
“Instead, it chose to export death, terrorism, extremism, and instability against America and our allies. … The President took action to address the threat Iran poses to America and global stability,” he said.
Many Ohio Republican Congress members were quick to voice their support for the military attacks, which the Trump administration is calling Operation Epic Fury. 
“After rejecting repeated opportunities for a peaceful, diplomatic solution, Iran now faces decisive action from President Trump that eliminates this threat and defends American security,” Ohio Republican U.S. Rep. Troy Balderson, of Zanesville, said on X. 
Trump is showing that the U.S. will not be intimidated by foreign threats, Ohio Republican U.S. Rep. Dave Joyce, of Novelty. 
“The U.S. is targeting Iranian military assets to degrade the regime’s ability to acquire nuclear weapons and threaten our national security and our allies in the region,” Joyce said on X. 
Ohio Republican U.S. Rep. Bob Latta, of Bowling Green, said Trump acted decisively. 
“Iran remains the world’s leading state sponsor of terrorism and has continued to pursue its relentless nuclear ambitions,” Latta said on X. 
“In response to Iran’s nuclear weapons development, President Trump and his Administration have worked to pursue peace agreements while prioritizing American security.” 
Follow OCJ Reporter Megan Henry on X.

name: Ohio Capital Journal
title: Ohio lawmaker queue up data center study commission
description: Ohio lawmakers appear ready to set up a new commission studying data centers. Business groups believe it can vindicate the industry, while ordinary Ohioans worry time is short to rein in the already out-of-control growth of data centers in the state. And if there were any doubt that data centers will be a potent campaign […]
text:
An Amazon Web Services data center is shown situated near single-family homes. (Photo by Nathan Howard/Getty Images)Ohio lawmakers appear ready to set up a new commission studying data centers. Business groups believe it can vindicate the industry, while ordinary Ohioans worry time is short to rein in the already out-of-control growth of data centers in the state.
And if there were any doubt that data centers will be a potent campaign issue in this year’s elections, a pair of political candidates recently took the opportunity to criticize lawmakers for not doing more.
After a House Committee voted to advance the proposal Tuesday, the bill seems likely to go before the full chamber Wednesday.
What the study looks like
Ohio state Rep. Gary Click, R-Vickery, said the motive behind forming a study commission is the opposite of the one driving most legislation.
“Typically, when we have legislation, we’re starting with an answer,” Click said. “It’s like, we know this is the answer to this problem, but a commission starts with the questions — what are the issues that we face? So, let’s take in all of the data, and then we’ll write something afterwards.”
Click is co-sponsoring Ohio House Bill 646 with Ohio state Rep. Kellie Deeter, R-Norwalk.
Their proposal would set up a 13-member study commission made up primarily of state representatives and senators. A last-minute amendment rejiggered the commission membership. The changes mandate specific subject matter expertise among members but potentially dilute minority party representation.
Gov. Mike Dewine gets to appoint three members, and the House Speaker and Senate President get five appointees each.
The latest version of the bill requires appointees familiar with data center operations, local governments, public utilities and tax incentive policy among others. Under the original proposal, minority leaders each got to appoint two out of those five members from their respective chambers. Now the measure only requires one member come from the minority party.
A handful of Democratic members voted against advancing the bill out of committee. After the hearing Rep. Ismail Mohamed, D-Columbus, explained they were frustrated with a slower, deliberative response when their constituents see an immediate problem.
“Commissions are things that we would typically vote yes on, because we have no issue with commissions,” he said. “But the issue is, right now, when it comes to data centers, a lot of them have been popping up, and they are having a severe impact on localities. They’re moving really fast.”
The bill requires at least four public hearings. Two of them must include expert testimony and two more open the floor for public input.
The commission’s task is enumerated in statute. The members must consider economic, environmental, agricultural, and utility impacts. They also have to look at potential pollution, including from wastewater, noise, and light.
Other priorities are more diffuse: How do data centers and their underlying technologies relate to national security? Are foreign actors spreading propaganda meant to foster opposition to data centers?
And the Data Center Study Commission will be working on a tight timeline. If it passes, the first hearing must be held within 45 days of the law taking effect, and commissioners must submit a report within six months of the effective date.
What Ohioans had to say
Kara Hetrick lives on 15 wooded acres outside Gibsonburg in Northwest Ohio. She’s frustrated that a proposed data center could go up less than a mile from her home.
“What happens in 10 to 15 years when the data center leaves the area?” she asked during a hearing last week.
Hetrick thinks the constant hum and bright lights will scare off local wildlife, and she worries the water used to cool the facility will get contaminated and eventually wind up in a nearby lake.
But she was particularly perturbed about the commission studying whether foreign propaganda is fueling data center opposition.
“Now we’re going to have more wasteful tax dollars to study a so-called foreign propaganda,” she said, “when really all it is, (is) we the people voicing our concerns with opposition.”
Nikki Gerber runs a canoe rental company in Manchester, Ohio and described her Adams County home in glowing terms.
“Magic comes to mind,” she said.
Gerber hopes the commission can provide guardrails to protect the area’s natural resources.
“Because the way they have been operating is stripping us of everything those before us learned from living through the industrial development of the past,” she said.
Perhaps hinting at data centers’ political salience on the campaign trail, a pair of politicians made sure to offer their two cents as well.
Eric Watson is challenging Rep. Click in this year’s Republican primary.
Watson insisted he came not as a candidate but as a concerned citizen.
He opposed the measure and criticized lawmakers for not including outside experts on the commission. He warned electromagnetic radiation could harm pollinators like honeybees, and that local governments are getting sued into submission by tech companies.
Kim Georgeton, who’s running for lieutenant governor alongside Republican Casey Putsch, argued that studying the issue doesn’t go nearly far enough.
“I’m asking you to adopt an immediate moratorium for a minimum of 36 months on data centers, server farms, and related AI infrastructure statewide,” she told the committee.
She insisted she’s not anti-tech — data centers just haven’t proven to be a good deal yet. They get big tax breaks, push power costs higher, and offer very few permanent jobs in return, she said.
“A moratorium on data centers is not a no, it’s just a not yet,” Georgeton said.
The business view
Meanwhile, the Ohio Chamber of Commerce sees a booming industry bringing jobs and tax revenue to the state.
Chamber Senior Vice President Rick Carfagna told lawmakers data centers were responsible for more than 95,000 jobs in 2024 and $26.4 billion in economic output, according to Chamber research.
Notably, that figure includes temporary jobs related to construction.
While some Ohioans are mounting a campaign to abolish property taxes, Carfagna added, “data centers represent a significant source of local government revenue that does not come from constituent wallets.”
He noted that’s about $1billion in state and local taxes that support schools, libraries, and local governments “without asking more from homeowners and small businesses.”
Greg Lawson from the free market think tank The Buckeye Institute, framed his argument in national security terms.
He argued that the country is engaged in a kind of “tech Cold War” with China, and the artificial intelligence tools involved in that stand-off rely on data centers.
But The Ohio Farm Bureau sounded a more skeptical tone.
The group’s Director of State Policy Evan Callicoat explained that his members are torn: Modern agricultural technology is crucial to their success, but the scope of land getting committed to data centers is troubling.
“Once farmland is lost, it’s likely gone forever,” Callicoat explained, and many of his members aren’t sure the benefits data centers promise outweigh the costs.
“Establishing a study commission enables an in-depth cost benefit analysis to ensure we do what is best for our state in the long term before we reach a breaking point,” he said.
Follow Ohio Capital Journal Reporter Nick Evans on X or on Bluesky.

name: Ohio Capital Journal
title: Appeals court strikes down Ohio law requiring burial of abortion remains
description: An Ohio appellate court sided with abortion rights advocates in an appeal that blocks most of a law that regulated how clinics dispose of remains from abortions. The case came to the First District Court of Appeals after Ohio Senate Bill 27 was passed in 2020, requiring the burial of fetal or embryonic remains after […]
text:
(Photo by Roberto Machado Noa/Getty Images)An Ohio appellate court sided with abortion rights advocates in an appeal that blocks most of a law that regulated how clinics dispose of remains from abortions.
The case came to the First District Court of Appeals after Ohio Senate Bill 27 was passed in 2020, requiring the burial of fetal or embryonic remains after an abortion.
The bill was almost immediately taken to court, and a Hamilton County Court of Common Pleas judge blocked the law.
The appellate court upheld most of the trial court’s decision, striking down all but two provisions of the law.
S.B. 27 was passed to require clinics that conduct abortion to cremate and inter fetal tissue or remains from an abortion at their own cost.
The law also created criminal liability for facilities conducting abortions who violated the law, and said surgical abortions couldn’t occur without clinicians first notifying the pregnant person of their right to choose the method of remains disposal.
Exceptions in the case of medical emergency were included in the law.
But the law was written before Ohio voters amended the state constitution to protect the right to abortion and other reproductive health.
The amendment passed by 57% of voters in 2023 also barred the state from creating any measure that would “burden, penalize, or discriminate against” those wanting an abortion or helping others receive one.
SUBSCRIBE: GET THE MORNING HEADLINES DELIVERED TO YOUR INBOX
In the case of the fetal remains law, the appellate court pointed to the constitutional amendment, particularly the provisions that specifically mentioned abortion.
“As written, subsection (A) of the Reproductive Freedom Amendment sets forth the affirmative right to an abortion for all individuals,” the three-judge panel stated in its decision last week.
The judges went on to say, “Ohio voters said what they meant.”
In its appeal, the state of Ohio argued that the law focused specifically on actions after an abortion was completed, therefore was not in conflict with the constitutional amendment.
The court disagreed with the state, saying that though the actions did occur after the fact, they were directly connected to the abortion and thus protected under the rights given in the Ohio Constitution, including from overly restrictive state measures.
“The plain language of the amendment applies to government action that affects all phases of reproductive decision-making, including discrimination that might occur after a procedure,” the decision stated.
“The mere fact that S.B. 27 regulates conduct occurring after an abortion does not remove it from the scope of the Reproductive Freedom Amendment.”
While the appeals court judges agreed with the Hamilton County court that the law flies in the face of the state constitution, they disagreed that the entire law should be thrown out.
The ruling allowed the state to keep two provisions of the law, one that updates the definition of the term “probable gestational age” to include the terms “zygote” and “blastocyte,” and another that requires a physician to create individual abortion reports for the Ohio Department of Health to include abortions involving these “organisms.”
The provision also requires the department to include “zygotes” and “blastocytes” in its data on abortions.
Amid its decision largely against S.B. 27, the three-judge panel noted that the constitutional amendment does give the right to abortion, but does not make that right “absolute.”
The court ruling said that while the amendment “significantly constrains the state’s ability to regulate in the field of abortion,” it also clearly allows the state to retain authority “to enact abortion related statutes, so long as it can demonstrate that doing so is ‘the lease restrictive means to advance the individual’s health in accordance with widely accepted and evidence-based standards of care,’” quoting language from the amendment itself.
The court also noted amendment language that bans abortion up to fetal viability, a provision of the amendment that specifies viability should be determined by an individual’s physician.
“These two exceptions carve out significant space for the state to operate,” the ruling stated. “And the voters approved the amendment with the specific understanding that the state had some leeway to regulate and even ban abortion going forward.”
Still, the judges said the amendment’s core purpose was to “protect reproductive decisions and activities from state interference absent a real health and safety concern.”
Anti-abortion organization Ohio Right to Life criticized the decision, claiming the amendment “is being used far beyond what any voter would imagine.”
“It’s unfortunate, but not a surprise, that the First District Court of Appeals sided with the abortion industry to stop Ohio’s fetal remains law from taking effect,” wrote Ohio Right to Life Executive Director Carrie Snyder.
The ACLU of Ohio represented reproductive health clinics in the case, and praised the decision as “yet another historic application” of the constitution amendment.
“While this law has not been in effect for years, today’s ruling will allow our clients to focus on providing essential health care without further interference from the state,” said Jessie Hill, cooperating attorney for the ACLU of Ohio.

name: Maine Morning Star
title: Mainers shouldn’t have to wait for eye care
description: I was born and raised in Presque Isle, where access to health care can be limited. After graduating from optometry school in Boston, I returned to my home state of Maine, where I now live and practice in Kittery. My goal as an optometrist has always been to provide the best care to my patients […]
text:
Maine legislators are considering expanding the definition of optometric practice, allowing optometrists in Maine to provide a limited set of in-office procedures. (Photo by Science Photo Library/ Getty Images)I was born and raised in Presque Isle, where access to health care can be limited. After graduating from optometry school in Boston, I returned to my home state of Maine, where I now live and practice in Kittery. My goal as an optometrist has always been to provide the best care to my patients by utilizing all knowledge and training I received in optometry school.
Today, Maine’s legislators have the opportunity to continue putting patients first by expanding access to important vision and medical eye care. Because optometry is a regulated profession in Maine, state lawmakers, like their counterparts in other states, determine the scope of practice for health care providers, regardless of their training and education. Decades ago, Maine’s optometrists weren’t authorized to dilate pupils or prescribe eye drops for something as common as pink eye. Today that seems unimaginable.
Optometrists complete a four-year, doctoral level degree program at an accredited school of optometry. Our education and training include courses in anatomy, pharmacology, and neuroscience/neuro-ophthalmic disease. We learn how to diagnose and treat glaucoma, cataracts and other diseases, how to use advanced treatment methods including non-invasive lasers, and how to perform minor eye care procedures to remove bumps and skin tags on eyelids.
Although these procedures are taught in optometry school and are allowed to be performed by optometrists in other states, they currently can only be performed by ophthalmologists in Maine. I am residency-trained and a fellow of the American Academy of Optometry, a distinction awarded to optometrists who have met rigorous professional standards. I practice in a medical-based optometry clinic where our doctors have multiple board certifications. We are the only optometry practice in Kittery and Wells and serve patients from southern Maine and coastal New Hampshire. When patients need care that we are not legally allowed to provide, we must refer them to outside providers, even though we have the training and education to provide this care. Too often that means long drives, few appointment options, repetitive testing, insurance barriers, and additional costs.
Access to care should not be about whether a service exists but about whether patients can realistically receive it. We currently do not have any glaucoma specialists in southern Maine, and the ophthalmologists in Portland don’t have room in their schedules to see new glaucoma patients. In addition, if a provider doesn’t accept a patient’s insurance, they are forced to travel even farther for care that is covered by their plan. This is not good news for Mainers.
If optometrists were able to practice to the full extent of their education and training, they could perform a procedure that is accepted as the first line or preferred treatment for glaucoma. That procedure, called selective laser trabeculoplasty or SLT, helps treat glaucoma by reducing pressure in the eye. It is more cost effective and can eliminate the need for daily eye drops. Another procedure, called YAG capsulotomy, is used to remove a thin film that commonly develops on the lens after cataract surgery. A third procedure, called laser peripheral iridotomy or LPI, can provide urgent treatment when pressure in the eye must be reduced immediately. All of these procedures can be completed in an optometrist’s office using the same machines we use every day and do not require the use of general anesthesia. They also provide immediate results and allow patients to quickly resume regular activities.
Having the ability to perform these procedures means we can offer more comprehensive, timely care to our patients. These procedures will become more in demand since Maine’s population is aging, with more than 22% of residents over age 65. This is the highest percentage of any state and is straining access to health care services, including eye care.
There have been multiple optometric scope expansions in Maine over the years. History shows that any time scope legislation is discussed, opponents predict dangerous outcomes for patients—but safety records do not support that. The first priority of optometrists is, and always will be, patient safety. Changing regulations will not change that.
Vision problems can quickly impact daily life. Mainers deserve access to safe vision and eye care without long waits for appointments or lengthy travel. It’s time to take the next step toward modernizing optometric care in Maine.

name: Kansas Reflector
title: Kansas anti-trans law targets IDs and birth certificates — and the right to vote
description: Last week, Kansas enacted another in a growing string of anti-transgender laws — and this latest legislation threatens transgender people’s right to vote. The new law, House Substitute for Senate Bill 244, is another example of increasingly extreme government overreach and efforts to restrict transgender people’s rights nationwide. In addition to a sweeping[...]
text:
Kansans rally in support of transgender rights May 5, 2023, at the Statehouse in Topeka. (Sherman Smith/Kansas Reflector)Last week, Kansas enacted another in a growing string of anti-transgender laws — and this latest legislation threatens transgender people’s right to vote.
The new law, House Substitute for Senate Bill 244, is another example of increasingly extreme government overreach and efforts to restrict transgender people’s rights nationwide. In addition to a sweeping and dangerous bathroom ban, SB 244 requires any Kansas birth certificate or driver’s license that reflects a transgender person’s gender identity to be invalidated and revoked — a first in the nation.
The law further requires transgender Kansans to surrender their now-invalid driver’s license to the state before they can receive a replacement — one that will only reflect their sex at birth, regardless of how these Kansans may look or live their life today, and regardless of the risks to their own safety.
This law was enacted despite the Legislature’s anti-democratic tactics to avoid public hearings; despite the fact that courts have ruled previous Kansas restrictions on updating gender markers on IDs to be unconstitutional; and despite the fact that when transgender people are forced to use IDs that do not reflect their gender identity, they are more likely to face harassment, discrimination, and even violence.
While this new, wide-sweeping law has many dangerous potential consequences, a particularly troubling one is its threat to transgender Kansans’ ability to exercise their constitutional right to vote.
This is because Kansas has one of the strictest voter ID laws in the country. Kansas requires that voters present a state-issued, unexpired, photo ID to cast a ballot. If a voter is unable to provide an ID that meets these requirements, they must cast a provisional ballot, which will only be counted if the voter submits an accepted ID to their county elections office by the Monday following the election.
These two policies together mean that many transgender Kansans may have their IDs invalidated this year yet still be required to produce a valid ID to be allowed to vote. This combination means that at least some of an estimated 22,000 adult transgender Kansans will face serious obstacles to voting this fall and beyond.
As Gov. Laura Kelly noted in her veto of the bill, it is “poorly drafted,” creating many unanswered questions that further illustrate the threat to transgender Kansans’ ability to vote. There is no information, process or timeline offered for how the state will revoke these IDs, or when affected Kansans will be notified that their IDs are now invalid and unusable. There is no information about whether affected Kansans will be forced to pay for new IDs.
There are no measures offered to ensure that transgender Kansans will not be turned away by confused poll workers who see an ID with a gender marker that does not reflect the person standing before them. There are certainly no measures offered to minimize the risk that transgender Kansans will avoid voting entirely, out of reasonable fear that showing mismatched IDs at the polls may expose them to mistreatment.
This policy is not only discriminatory: It’s voter suppression.
IDs unlock essential freedoms we all rely on — like the freedom to travel, work, drive and more. Revoking a transgender person’s ID after it has been issued via a rigorous, state supervised process — and then banning future updates — strips those freedoms away.
Every Kansan, including transgender Kansans, deserves to vote free from mistreatment and unnecessary obstacles. We should be encouraging more civic participation, not making it harder for Americans to vote. SB 244, however, does just that: it not only  threatens transgender Kansans’ basic safety and freedom, but also their very right to participate in the democratic process.
As the director of policy research at the Movement Advancement Project, Logan S. Casey (he/him) offers expertise in laws and policies that impact the lived experiences of LGBTQ+ people. Through its opinion section, Kansas Reflector works to amplify the voices of people who are affected by public policies or excluded from public debate. Find information, including how to submit your own commentary, here.

name: Ohio Capital Journal
title: Ranked choice voting favors moderate candidates over divisive ones. Ohio lawmakers want to ban it
description: If every state representative or state senator in Ohio had to receive more than 50% of all votes cast in an election to win, would the General Assembly have gutted a voter-approved marijuana law, outlawed diversity efforts in higher education, waged unrelenting war against LGBTQ+ Ohioans, or defiled Ohio’s state parks with pollution-generating oil and […]
text:
Getty Images file photo of a voter registration form.If every state representative or state senator in Ohio had to receive more than 50% of all votes cast in an election to win, would the General Assembly have gutted a voter-approved marijuana law, outlawed diversity efforts in higher education, waged unrelenting war against LGBTQ+ Ohioans, or defiled Ohio’s state parks with pollution-generating oil and gas drillers? 
I could go on. But think about it. 
If Ohio’s gerrymandered lawmakers could no longer skate into office on a plurality/winner-takes-all vote (with all-but-certain election results) and instead needed the majority support of the voting population to assume power under ranked choice voting, would that change the political calculus of legislative clowns impervious to the will of the people? 
Ohio House votes to stamp out ranked choice voting before it begins

A voting reform proposal that requires a winning candidate to be acceptable to at least 50% of voters — recently blocked by skittish state lawmakers — might mean less job security for politicians who regularly ignore clear public wishes on legalized recreational weed, abortion rights, academic freedom, environmental protection, commonsense gun reform, etc. 
Representational indifference may be shrugged off by a fraction of a fraction of diehard partisans in contorted districts where they have outsized leverage by design, but not a broader base of the electorate enraged by oblivious office holders. 
If reelection in Ohio depended on receiving more than half of the total votes cast, instead a small percentage of the overall vote in elections decided in primaries, would culture war grandstanding in the Ohio Statehouse lose to outcome-based policymaking? 
Would ranked choice voting — a modest, procedural upgrade in the election system that allows voters to rank candidates for office in order of their preferences — strengthen the quality of candidates running while also centering voters? 
Ohioans may never know. Why? Because the possibility of ranked choice voting being used in statewide elections has been preemptively banned in Ohio to protect the aforementioned gerrymandered lawmakers. 
Ohio Senate Bill 63, passed in the Ohio Senate last May and last week by the Ohio House, barred ranked ranked choice voting in statewide elections and threatened to withhold funding from any Ohio city that dare implement it under its home rule prerogative —  which several municipalities are considering. 
Former Democratic state senator and Ohio attorney general Marc Dann promoted the change in Lakewood. 
“If you want a government that’s more inclusive and less vitriolic, this (ranked choice voting) is a solution that can be used to get there,” he said. 
Under ranked choice voting, people pick their favorite candidate knowing that if he or she doesn’t gather enough votes to win, their vote will count toward their second favorite or third until one candidate has a majority of top rankings.
The voting model to encourage more voter engagement, more diverse candidates, and more policy-focused campaigns to generate broad-based appeal, has been around for over a century and was once widely used in Ohio from Ashtabula to Cincinnati. 
It is how Americans vote today in some cities and states. 
“Most people like ranked choice voting once they use it,” said Denise Riley who heads the nonpartisan Rank the Vote Ohio nonprofit. 
“That’s why we would love to see our cities use it so the public can see how it works. I’m sure that’s why state lawmakers wanted to ban it in the cities” before it gets a foothold in communities that recognize its benefits over the status quo election system. 
The registered Republican noted that ranked choice voting favors moderate candidates over polarizing ones that just sling mud.
SUBSCRIBE: GET THE MORNING HEADLINES DELIVERED TO YOUR INBOX
It also fosters a wide-ranging candidate pool to bring new perspectives to a race and reinvigorate competition with ideas that actually resonate with a majority of voters. 
But reform that gives voters “a greater choice, a stronger voice, and a representative democracy for all Ohioans,” can unseat entrenched partisans clinging to “safe seats” on a limited base of support. 
So, of course, those entrenched partisans in the legislature squashed ranked choice voting in the state before it could start.
The prospects of foregone conclusions in elections being replaced by competitive races decided by the entire electorate — not just a passionate base — could ruin everything gerrymandering has done to seal control.
Ohio Republicans owe their dominion in the legislature to rigged redistricting. Ranked choice voting is a threat to their engineered supermajorities in the Ohio Senate and Ohio House. 
The go-to GOP flack in the Senate for disempowering and disenfranchising Ohio voters, Bowling Green Republican Theresa Gavarone, helped neutralize that threat with Senate Bill 63. 
She gave the party’s ranked choice voting ban a bipartisan sheen by enlisting feckless Columbus Democrat Bill DeMora as co-sponsor. 
A smattering of spineless Democrats in both chambers followed suit, choosing self-preservation over an arguably more representative voting process not even in use in Ohio. 
They discarded opposition testimony on S.B. 63 that overwhelmed out-of-state proponents aligned with the Heritage Foundation. 
That’s how much insulated politicos feared losing power to more broadly popular competitors preferred by majority consensus.
“Wherever ranked choice is proposed,” said Riley, “the party in power, whether Republican or Democrat, is opposed to it because it gives voters power and not the parties.” \
Politicians whine about ranked choice voting being too complicated or confusing as a smokescreen to conceal the truth, she continued. 
“Ranked choice voting takes away some of their power to do whatever they want” and “Ohio is ripe for (ranked choice voting),” Riley predicted. 
A state government willfully and repeatedly blind to the demands of the governed will compel change.

name: The Texas Tribune
title: Here’s what we learned from the action-packed Texas primaries
description: The state’s two blockbuster Senate primaries dominated the airwaves, and further down the ballot, chaos reigned. The lack of decisive outcomes promises an equally intense runoff season.

GOP race for Arkansas secretary of state appears headed to runoff


By: Andrew DeMillo - Wednesday March 4, 2026 12:38 am





Voters cast their ballots during the primary election at Saint Mark Baptist Church in Little Rock, Arkansas on Tuesday, March 3, 2026. (Photo by Katie Adkins/Arkansas Advocate)
The three-person race for Arkansas secretary of state appears headed toward a runoff later this month, but it remained unclear late Tuesday which two candidates would advance.
State Sen. Kim Hammer, Army veteran Bryan Norris and Miller County Judge Cathy Hardin Harrison each had about a third of the vote with about 94% of votes in by late Tuesday night. Since none of the candidates won the majority needed to claim the nomination outright, the top two will advance to a March 31 runoff.
The three are running to replace Secretary of State Cole Jester, who won the GOP primary for land commissioner. Whoever wins the GOP nomination will face Democrat Kelly Grappe in the fall.









Justice Nick Bronni defeats John Adams in Arkansas Supreme Court race


By: Ainsley Platt -  12:23 am





Arkansas Supreme Court Justice Nick Bronni (Photo courtesy of Arkansas Secretary of State)
Justice Nicholas Bronni has defeated private attorney John Adams to win a full term on the Arkansas Supreme Court.
Bronni won the state with nearly 55% of the vote to Adams’ 45%, according to The Associated Press, with more than 95% of the estimated vote counted.
Bronni was appointed to the court by Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders in 2024, barring him from running for the seat he currently holds. Instead, he ran for Justice Cody Hiland’s seat. Hiland, another appointee, ran for Bronni’s unopposed.
Though judicial races are nonpartisan in Arkansas, the Republican Party endorsed Bronni at the end of 2025. His win Tuesday is a victory for the state party after the last Arkansas Supreme Court candidate it endorsed, Justice Rhonda Wood, lost her bid to be chief justice of the court to now-Chief Justice Karen Baker in 2024.
Bronni is a former Arkansas solicitor general and self-described conservative who campaigned on his legal fights with the Biden administration and simplifying state courts to make them easier for the average Arkansan to use.
Bronni’s win cements the 5-2 conservative tilt of the Supreme Court, which settles state constitutional matters and is the final court of appeal for cases in the Arkansas’ legal system. The court also oversees the administration of the state’s 28 circuit courts and the Arkansas Court of Appeals, which collectively saw more than 180,000 cases filed in 2025.

 Last updated: 12:24 am 







Russell wins Democratic nomination to challenge U.S. Rep. Bruce Westerman


By: Tess Vrbin -  12:15 am





James Russell III (Courtesy of the Arkansas Secretary of State)
Democrat James Russell III will challenge Republican U.S. Rep. Bruce Westerman to represent Arkansas’ 4th Congressional District in November’s general election.
Russell defeated Steve O’Donnell by more than 1,500 votes in Tuesday’s Democratic primary. The Associated Press called the race for Russell just after midnight Wednesday morning with 97% of votes counted. Russell received 53% of the vote over O’Donnell’s 47%.
Russell, of Little Rock, runs the business side of his wife’s mental health counseling practice. He has said his work informs his advocacy for universal single-payer health care.
He entered politics in 2022 by seeking the Democratic nomination for governor, losing to Chris Jones, who became the Democratic nominee for Arkansas’ 2nd Congressional District Tuesday.
Westerman, of Hot Springs, is a forester who chairs the House Natural Resources Committee. He was first elected to Congress in 2014.
The 4th District contains parts of Pulaski and Sebastian counties and the entirety of 31 more counties from the state’s southern and western borders to part of northern Arkansas.









King wins primary, beating Sanders-backed challenger for Senate seat


By: Ainsley Platt - Tuesday March 3, 2026 11:42 pm





Republican Sen. Bryan King of Green Forest beat a primary opponent Tuesday who had been backed by the governor he routinely criticizes, setting him up for reelection in November.
Sen. Bryan King and Amber Sullivan (center), daughter of the late Sen. Gary Stubblefield, criticized Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders at a press conference on Sept. 30, 2025, for scheduling a special election for District 26 for next June, leaving it unrepresented during Arkansas’ fiscal session next year. Stubblefield died Sept. 2. (Photo by Ainsley Platt/Arkansas Advocate)
King defeated Bobby Ballinger, who Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders endorsed and campaigned for as part of her efforts to oust two incumbent Republican senators who had voted against approving construction funds for a controversial state prison in Franklin County.
Ballinger is the son of former Sen. Bob Ballinger, who King defeated in the 2022 election.
King came away with 60% of the vote with all precincts in the district fully reporting, according to unofficial results on the Arkansas secretary of state’s website.
With no Democrats running in November’s general election, the win all but ensures King’s reelection.
King regularly breaks with his own party on matters prioritized by Sanders, and he frequently accuses Sanders and her staff of being fiscally irresponsible in their plans.
Like with the Sanders-backed challenge to fellow Republican incumbent Sen. Ron Caldwell, the campaign against King was aided by mailers paid for by outside groups that painted the senator from Green Forest as insufficiently conservative. Caldwell dealt a resounding defeat to his challenger, with 72% of the vote according to the Associated Press.
King was a “no” vote on Franklin County prison funding, and he said Sanders’ backing of his opponent was because he could not be controlled. Sanders has described the Franklin County prison as necessary to relieve longstanding overcrowding issues in state prisons that’s led to a back-up of people in county jails waiting for a spot in a state facility.









Wooldridge unseats Senate majority leader in Republican primary for Senate District 21


By: Antoinette Grajeda -  11:34 pm





Rep. Jeremy Wooldridge of Marmaduke defeated Senate Majority Leader Blake Johnson of Corning in the Republican primary for the Senate District 21 seat.
Rep. Jeremy Wooldridge (Courtesy of the Arkansas Secretary of State)
Wooldridge won the election with 62% of the vote, according to unofficial but complete results from the Arkansas secretary of state. 
Wooldridge is serving his second term in the Arkansas House. He has represented House District 1, which includes all of Clay and portions of Greene and Randolph counties, since being elected in 2022. 
The Marmaduke Republican serves as vice chair of the House Public Health, Welfare and Labor Committee. 
Wooldridge’s election could have an impact on who replaces outgoing Senate president pro tempore Bart Hester. Johnson, who’s serving his second term as majority leader, had said he planned to run for the position. 
Wooldridge does not have a Democratic or Libertarian candidate in November’s general election. He will begin a four-year term in the Senate in January. 









Achor, Sweatman win primaries for North Little Rock area Senate seat


By: Andrew DeMillo -  11:31 pm





Allison Grigsby Sweatman attends the Democratic watch party on Tuesday, March 3, 2026 in Little Rock, Arkansas. (Photo by Katie Adkins/Arkansas Advocate)
Voters in North Little Rock, Sherwood, Maumelle and part of Jacksonville will have a choice between Republican Rep. Brandon Achor and returning Democratic candidate Allison Grigsby Sweatman in November’s state Senate race.
Rep. Brandon Achor (Courtesy of the Arkansas Secretary of State)
Unofficial results from the Pulaski County Election Commission show Sweatman defeated Jason Williams in the Democratic primary by 461 votes and Achor defeated Mischa Martin in the GOP primary by 484 votes.
Achor is a pharmacist in his second state House term. Sweatman is a social worker and former Young Democrats of Arkansas president. She lost the 2022 race for the same seat to GOP Sen. Jane English, who is not seeking reelection due to term limits.

 Last updated: 11:32 pm

Arkansas Democrats flip House District 70 in special election


By: Antoinette Grajeda -  10:11 pm





Arkansas Democrats flipped a legislative seat in North Little Rock Tuesday when Democrat Alex Holladay defeated Republican Bo Renshaw in the House District 70 special election. 
Alex Holladay (Courtesy of the Arkansas Secretary of State)
Holladay won the race with nearly 60% of the vote.
The special election was called to fill the vacancy left by Republican Rep. Carlton Wing who resigned last year to run the state’s public television station. Holladay previously lost to Wing by about 300 votes in his 2024 bid for the same seat. 
Holladay will serve the remainder of Wing’s term, which ends in January. 
The newly-elected Democrat thanked his supporters in a social media post and said he was ready to get to work and would attend legislative meetings Wednesday. 
“The People are ready to hold our state’s leaders accountable, and that’s what this election was about,” Holladay said. “Thank you for trusting me with your vote. With my new colleagues, I will work tirelessly to make good on my promise to center the cost of living and government transparency in my work.”
Holladay also won the HD 70 Democratic primary. He earned a victory over Cordelia Smith-Johnson with nearly 83% of the vote. Holladay will face Renshaw again in November’s general election, and the winner will begin a two-year term in the House in January.









Caldwell wins reelection to state Senate against challenger backed by governor


By: Ainsley Platt -  10:08 pm





Sen. Ronald Caldwell (Photo courtesy of Arkansas Secretary of State)
Republican Sen. Ron Caldwell resoundingly defeated a primary challenger backed by members of his own party, effectively winning reelection to the Arkansas Senate.
Caldwell, a longtime member of the legislature, was in the crosshairs of Trey “BoDirt” Bohannan, a fellow Republican backed by Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders and Kid Rock, in one of two races testing the governor’s influence over the predominantly GOP Legislature.
But Caldwell dealt Bohannan a decisive defeat, earning 72% of the vote with an estimated 87% of votes counted after the Associated Press called the race. No Democrat is running for the seat in the fall.
Since Sanders took office in 2023, Caldwell opposed several of the governor’s priorities — he was one of a handful of Republican senators who voted against funding the construction of the controversial Franklin County prison, and he has expressed reservations about the LEARNS Act, Sanders’ signature education bill.
The race was unusually acrimonious for a primary involving an incumbent, with campaign mailers painting Caldwell as insufficiently conservative paid for by outside groups blanketing the district. Sanders actively campaigned for Bohannan, even after the candidate attracted controversy for his business relationship with a man convicted of internet stalking women and girls.
The Senate race drawn an odd assortment of characters, with singer Kid Rock recording a video endorsing Bohannon’s bid.
Bohannan tried to characterize his opponent as President Donald Trump’s “worst enemy in the state Senate,” and several of the mailers did the same. Votes on bills that Caldwell either missed or declined to cast a vote on were turned into attacks for not supporting anti-abortion laws or allowing undue Chinese influence in Arkansas.
But despite the attacks fueled by outside groups’ involvement in the race, Caldwell was “very confident” in his chances hours before polls closed. Caldwell called the attacks on him a “character assassinations,” and attributed Sanders’ support for his opponent to his votes against funding the prison project.

 Last updated: 10:10 pm 







Republican Brad Simon wins Arkansas Senate seat at center of prison debate


By: Ainsley Platt -  10:02 pm





Brad Simon (Courtesy of the Arkansas Secretary of State)
Republican Brad Simon defeated independent candidate Adam Watson in the special Senate election Tuesday to determine the late Sen. Gary Stubblefield’s successor.
The Associated Press called the race with an estimated 80% of the vote counted, with Simon earning 69% of the vote to Watson’s 31%.
With the win, Simon is set to represent the district that’s the site of controversial 3,000 bed prison. Simon will serve in the Senate in this year’s fiscal session, when a debate for funding over the prison is expected.
The project has stirred local opposition in Senate District 26, which is comprised of parts of Franklin, Logan, Sebastian and Johnson counties. Every candidate who ran for the seat vowed to oppose it being built in Franklin County, on the 815-acre plot of land purchased by the state for the prison in 2024.
Watson hoped to pull off an upset by leveraging district voters’ discontent with Sanders and state Republicans over the Franklin County prison. Before declaring to run in the race, Watson built up an online following around his opposition to the prison. 
He was also part of a group that spoke to a legislative committee  last year about their concerns with the site selection process for the 815-acre plot purchased by the state for the prison in 2024.
But he couldn’t overcome the district’s Republican preferences, or Simon’s large spending and fundraising advantages. 










Arkansas Secretary of State Cole Jester wins GOP primary for land commissioner


By: Andrew DeMillo -  9:23 pm





Secretary of State Cole Jester announced on Oct. 30, 2025 that his office identified 240 potential noncitizens on state voter rolls after feeding voter registration data into a federal database tool. (Photo by Ainsley Platt/Arkansas Advocate)
Secretary of State Cole Jester has won the Republican nomination for commissioner of state lands.
The Associated Press called the race for Jester with 53% of the vote counted Tuesday night.
Jester defeated Christian Olson in Tuesday’s primary to replace term-limited Land Commissioner Tommy Land. He will face Libertarian nominee Christopher Olson in the November election.
Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders in 2024 appointed Jester to serve as secretary of state after John Thurston won a special election to serve the remainder of the late Treasurer Mark Lowery’s term. Jester was unable to run for secretary of state since he was appointed to the post. 

 Last updated: 9:26 pm 







Shoffner wins Democratic nomination to challenge Sen. Tom Cotton


By: Tess Vrbin -  9:04 pm





Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Hallie Shoffner votes at the Pulaski County Regional Building on Feb. 23, 2026, accompanied by her 7-year-old son, Max Sullivan. (Photo by Tess Vrbin/Arkansas Advocate)
Arkansans will have a choice between Democratic newcomer Hallie Shoffner and two-term incumbent Republican U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton in November’s general election.
The Associated Press called the Democratic primary for Shoffner with 40% of the votes counted Tuesday night. She won 81.2% of the vote to Lewisville Mayor Ethan Dunbar’s 18.8%.
“I’m so humbled and grateful that the people of Arkansas have decided to put their trust in me, and I’m going to prove them right,” Shoffner told the Arkansas Advocate. “I’m going to take it to the mat every single day for them. That’s what this has been about since the beginning.”
Shoffner’s social media presence and her background as a farmer talking about the impact of Trump’s tariffs have garnered national attention. Her motivation to run came from last year’s closure of her family’s 2,000-acre soybean farm after six generations of farming in Jackson County.
Shoffner’s campaign received more than $1 million in contributions between July and December 2025. She had roughly $500,000 in cash on hand as of February.
Cotton, who chairs the Senate Intelligence Committee, has more than $9.6 million in the bank.

 Last updated: 10:15 pm 







Jones, Hill set to run for Arkansas’ 2nd U.S. House seat


By: Tess Vrbin -  8:44 pm





Chris Jones, a candidate for Arkansas’ 2nd Congressional District, takes a selfie outside the voting precinct at Immanuel Baptist Church’s City Center in Little Rock, Arkansas on Tuesday, March 3, 2026 (Photo by John Sykes/Arkansas Advocate)
Central Arkansas voters will have a choice between Democrat Chris Jones and Republican U.S. Rep. French Hill in November’s general election.
Republican U.S. Rep. French Hill (Photo courtesy of Arkansas Secretary of State)
Jones defeated Zackary Huffman in Tuesday’s Democratic primary for Arkansas’ 2nd Congressional District. The Associated Press called the race for Jones with 62% of the votes counted. Jones received 93.8% of the vote to Huffman’s 6.2%.
Jones is a pastor and physicist who grew up in Pine Bluff and lives in Little Rock. His first foray into politics was his unsuccessful bid for governor in 2022. He received 35% of the vote compared to Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders’ 63%.
Hill, also of Little Rock, chairs the House Financial Services Committee. He was first elected to Congress in 2014 and defeated primary challenger Chase McDowell on Tuesday. With 41% of votes counted, Hill had received 78.5% of the vote compared to McDowell’s 21.6%.
Hill is a former banker and a former aide to Arkansas Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders’ father, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee. His votes in Congress have typically aligned with President Donald Trump’s agenda during both of the president’s non-consecutive terms.
The 2nd District consists of most of Pulaski County and all of Saline, Faulkner, Perry, Van Buren, Cleburne and White counties.

 Last updated: 8:48 pm 







Love wins Democratic nomination for Arkansas governor


By: Antoinette Grajeda -  8:37 pm





State Sen. Fred Love, a Democrat from Mabelvale, votes at the Dee Brown Library with his 6-year-old daughter, Millicent Love, on Feb. 28, 2026. Love is running in the Democratic gubernatorial primary against Supha Xayprasith-Mays of Bentonville. (Photo by Tess Vrbin/Arkansas Advocate)
Sen. Fred Love will face Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders in November after defeating Supha Xayprasith-Mays in the Democratic primary election Tuesday night. 
The Associated Press called the race around 8:30 p.m. Tuesday. 
Xayprasith-Mays, a Bentonville businesswoman who ran an unsuccessful bid for the Democratic gubernatorial nomination in 2022, conceded the race and congratulated her opponent on his win. 
Both Love and Libertarian candidate Colt Shelby will face Sanders, who’s seeking a second term as governor, in the general election. 
Sanders, who was the first woman to be elected Arkansas governor in 2022, did not have an opponent for the Republican nomination Tuesday. If Love wins in November, he would become the first minority candidate to hold the office and the state’s first Democratic governor in over a decade. 
Sanders, who earned national attention as President Donald Trump’s White House press secretary, has amassed more than $4.1 million in campaign funds. Love, who has served in the state Legislature since 2011, has about $4,400. 










U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton of Arkansas wins Republican primary


By: Tess Vrbin -  8:30 pm










Republican U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton (right) meets employees of the L3Harris munitions plant in East Camden on Feb. 27, 2026. At left is Republican U.S. Rep. Bruce Westerman. (Photo by Tess Vrbin/Arkansas Advocate)


Two-term Republican U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton defeated two challengers in his primary election in Arkansas Tuesday.
The Associated Press called the race for Cotton with 20% of the votes counted. Cotton won 82.1 % of the vote over Micah Ashby’s 9.3% and Jeb Little’s 8.6%.
Cotton said on Facebook that he was honored to continue to have Republican voters’ trust.
“I’m running to lower costs for Arkansas families, to continue rebuilding our military and bringing jobs and investments home to Arkansas, and to make our state safer and more prosperous,” Cotton said. “I look forward to earning the support of Arkansans in November and continuing to be your voice in the Senate.”
Cotton has reported having more than $9.6 million in the bank for his reelection, a staggering amount compared to his rivals.
Closely aligned with President Donald Trump on issues like immigration, he also is widely viewed as a potential presidential hopeful in 2028.

Hallie Shoffner and Ethan Dunbar are running for the Democratic nomination to challenge Cotton.



 Last updated: 11:26 pm 







North Little Rock voters encounter long waits, frustration at one polling place


By: Tess Vrbin -  7:36 pm





Voters line up to vote in the partisan primaries and nonpartisan judicial elections in the gym at the Church of Christ in North Little Rock on March 3, 2026. (Photo by Tess Vrbin/Arkansas Advocate)
North Little Rock voters at the Church of Christ polling place said they were frustrated that it took more than an hour to vote Tuesday afternoon.
The line to vote in the gym stretched almost out the door, and the handful of election workers struggled to keep up with the crowd. Some voters left the line due to previous commitments or physical discomfort and said they would return later in the day.
Some said they were directed to vote at the church because there is no longer a polling location in North Little Rock’s Park Hill neighborhood after multiple polling districts were consolidated.
Rosemary Carden said she has lived in North Little Rock her entire life and never had trouble voting before Tuesday. 
She said she was angry that poll workers called her residence into question when she asked for both a Democratic primary ballot and the special general ballot for a Pulaski County state House seat. Someone else who lives at Carden’s address had no trouble voting on both ballots, she said.
Poll workers gave Carden a ballot with only one race on it, the Democratic primary for a North Little Rock House district in which Carden does not live, she said. She had been hoping to vote for Democrat Alex Holladay in the special general election, she said.
“Something ain’t right,” Carden said. “I don’t know what it is, but something’s not right.”
Jessica McCullough said it bothered her that the polling place did not have a Democratic sample ballot available but did have sample ballots for the Republican and nonpartisan primaries and the special House election.
McCullough did not say which primary ballot she chose but said the U.S. Senate and House primaries were among the most important matters on her ballot. She said she hadn’t anticipated the United States starting a war in the Middle East without Congressional approval shortly before the primaries.
“We just need a change,” she said. “I think we’re sitting on still waters and it’s tainting things.”
Republican U.S. Sen. Tom Cotton faces two primary challengers. In central Arkansas’ 2nd Congressional District, Republican Rep. French Hill has a primary challenger. The Democratic races to run for Cotton’s and Hill’s congressional seats each have two candidates.
McCullough said the state Supreme Court race between John Adams and Justice Nicholas Bronni was also important to her. She and Mark Danzy both declined to say for whom they voted in that race, but Danzy said he tends to vote against incumbent elected officials if he dislikes “the climate of the decisions that are being made in the state.”
“I’m not extremely happy with the climate at this time,” Danzy said.
Andrew Stone said he voted for Adams because he believes it’s important to “have somebody who can stand up to the governor on the Supreme Court and wouldn’t just follow a party line.” Bronni had been endorsed by the state Republican Party.










Election polls closed in Arkansas


By: Advocate Staff -  7:31 pm





Voters line up at the Pulaski Heights United Methodist Church for the primary election in Little Rock, Arkansas on Tuesday, March 3, 2026. (Photo by Katie Adkins/Arkansas Advocate)
Polls closed at 7:30 p.m. at polling locations around the state. Registered voters who were in line by 7:30 p.m. will still be allowed to vote.









In Franklin County, prison project is at the forefront for primary voters


By: Antoinette Grajeda -  6:09 pm





A sign in rural Franklin County protests plans for the state to build a new prison nearby on March 3, 2026. (Photo by Antoinette Grajeda/Arkansas Advocate)
A proposed 3,000-bed prison was top of mind for Franklin County voters casting their ballots in Charleston mid-afternoon Tuesday.
At a polling site on the Sacred Heart Catholic Church campus, located about seven miles south of the proposed prison site, voters like Melanie Neumeier were adamantly opposed to the project.
Neumeier said she selected a nonpartisan judicial ballot, which included the Senate District 26 special election. The seat became vacant last year following the death of Branch Republican Sen. Gary Stubblefield, a vocal opponent of the prison.
Neumeier said she voted for independent candidate Adam Watson in the Senate District 26 special election because he’s against the prison.
“We don’t want the prison here. We want somebody who hears us,” she said. “We want somebody who actually is transparent. We don’t want somebody who lies and wants that prison here when we don’t want it.”
Signs directly across from a proposed prison site in rural Franklin County protest the project on March 3, 2026. (Photo by Antoinette Grajeda/Arkansas Advocate)
Republican Brad Simon, who is running against Watson in the special election, has also said he’s opposed to locating the prison in Franklin County.
Neumeier said she doesn’t want the activity that comes with a prison in her community because it’s not good for Charleston. She said officials could put the prison in nearby Fort Smith or somewhere else.
Better reform programs are needed, not a new prison, she said.
“It’s just adding to the crime,” Neumeier said. “I mean, what are we going to do? Keep letting prisoners escape? We can’t even keep prisoners in the prisons we have. What are we gonna do? Add another one? It’s just ridiculous.”
William Hawley of Charleston said he supports President Trump and voted Republican “across the board” on Election Day, March 3, 2026. (Photo by Antoinette Grajeda/Arkansas Advocate)
William Hawley said he’s also “totally against” the prison, even though proponents have said it will bring jobs to the area.
“We don’t want those kind of jobs here,” he said. “[We] want to keep it nice and rural country Charleston.”
Republican Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders has said the prison is needed to address overcrowding. Hawley said she should look at expanding existing prisons or shipping prisoners to other states that have more room.
Hawley said he generally supports Sanders’ policies, but he splits with her on the prison project.
“If she’s going to support the prison, then I’m gonna have to support somebody else for governor because we don’t want that here,” he said. “She has the pull.”
